title: Turing patterns on non-fluctuating surfaces under mechanical stresses
authors: Fumitake Kato, Hiroshi Koibuchi, Madoka Nakayama, Sohei Tasaki, Tetsuya Uchimoto
abstract: This paper presents a numerical investigation of Turing patterns (TPs) utilizing the reaction-diffusion equation for the activator $u$ and the inhibitor $v$ on two- and three-dimensional lattices, discarding vertex fluctuations. The absence of vertex fluctuations means the absence of positional movement of $u$ and $v$. Consequently, $u$ and $v$ have values at spatially discrete points, such as the pigment cells in zebrafish and sea shells. Furthermore, the mechanical property is implemented through the Finsler geometry modeling technique. This technique incorporates the internal degree of freedom $\vecτ$, corresponding to the direction of mechanical stress. Additionally, a stress formula based on Gaussian bond potential is shown to be well-defined on the non-fluctuating lattices, and therefore, it enables the calculation of entropy for capturing the stress relaxation phenomenon in a manner analogous to that on fluctuating surfaces. The results of the study indicate that these biological patterns also exhibit responses to external mechanical forces similar to TPs on fluctuating membranes.
summary: This paper explores how Turing patterns form and align on rigid, non-fluctuating surfaces under mechanical strain. By using Finsler geometry, the authors model how mechanical stress influences the direction of pattern formation. They show that even without vertex fluctuations, the model successfully captures thermodynamic properties like entropy and stress relaxation. The results suggest that the behavior of patterns on rigid 3D structures is fundamentally similar to those on flexible 2D membranes.
main_problem: Investigating whether Turing patterns (TPs) on non-fluctuating (fixed) surfaces respond to external mechanical stresses in the same way as TPs on fluctuating membranes.
main_result: The study demonstrates that fixed-lattice models can accurately capture stress relaxation, entropy, and pattern anisotropy, showing that 3D rigid plates exhibit mechanical anisotropy analogous to 2D soft surfaces.
method: A hybrid numerical technique combining Monte Carlo simulations for surface variables and discrete Reaction-Diffusion (FitzHugh-Nagumo) equations for pattern dynamics.
model_or_system: A discrete Finsler geometry model on 2D (square, triangular) and 3D (cubic) lattices, utilizing a Gaussian bond potential and an internal degree of freedom representing the direction of mechanical stress.
key_observables: Tensile stress, surface tension, entropy density, free energy density, and order parameters for pattern direction correlation.
important_parameters: Fluctuation range (epsilon_R), deformation ratio (R), internal degree of freedom vector (tau), and diffusion/reaction coefficients.
main_assumptions: The model assumes non-fluctuating vertices (fixed positions) and treats the fixed lattice as the limit of a fluctuating lattice as epsilon_R approaches zero.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows biological pattern examples; Figure 2 illustrates lattice fluctuation ranges and partition function limits; Figure 5 shows TP snapshots on 2D/3D lattices; Figure 6 and 7 plot direction-dependent coefficients and energies; Figure 9 displays 3D plate stress, entropy, and free energy data; Figure 10 shows the discretization of the Finsler metric.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the biological motivation, defines the mathematical framework for the fixed-lattice model and Finsler geometry, details the hybrid simulation method, presents results for 2D and 3D lattice anisotropy, and concludes with thermodynamic analysis of stress relaxation.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1: Shell patterns that emerge during development can also be understood as an example of TP on solid materials, similar to TPs on soft materials, such as zebras and fish.

FIG. 2: The range εR of the vertex fluctuations defines the material property: (a) soft (⇔large εR) and (b) hard (⇔small εR), and (c) fixed lattice (⇔εR →0) with a deformed lattice spacing (Ra,a/R) with a deformation parameter R and a constant a. (d) An illustration of the compatibility relation for the zero fluctuation limit εR →0 and the calculation of physical quantity, where Z and Zfix denote the partition function of fluctuating and fixed lattices, respectively.

FIG. 3: The regular square and triangular lattices (a) and (b), respectively, where the total number of vertices is small, are plotted to elucidate the underlying structure. (c) The 3D cubic lattice of side lengths (Lx,Ly,Lz), where the total number of vertices is N =9600, is utilized in the simulations. The 2D lattices employ periodic boundary conditions (PBCs), while the 3D lattice’s PBCs are applied exclusively in the x, y directions.

FIG. 4: The TP direction is parallel to the⃗τ direction on the lattice extended along the x direction of (a) model 1 and (b) model 2. The magnitude of the unit Finsler length χG i1 along bond i1 depends on the model, thereby determining the⃗τ direction and the coupling constant ΓG i1, as shown in (c) and (d).

FIG. 5: Snapshots of TPs on (a), (b) and (c) 2D square lattices, (d), (e) and (f) 2D triangular lattices, and (g), (h) and (i) 3D cubic lattices. The parameters assumed in the simulations are shown in the plots. The small cones plotted at every second vertex represent the IDOF⃗τ. The⃗τ direction is nearly parallel (perpendicular) to that of TP in model 1 (model 2).

FIG. 6: (a) ΓG µ and ΓG µhµ 1 vs. R, (b) ΓG i j and h1 vs. R, and (c) the order parameters qµ τ vs. R of the 2D square lattice model. The data of the 2D triangular lattice model are plotted in (d), (e) and (f). The curved arrows ⟳and ⟲in (a) and (d) indicate the directional energy shift. The slight asymmetry observed in data (d) and (f) under the R ↔1/R transformation is due to the asymmetry of the triangular lattice structure along the x and y directions.
FIG. 7: (a) Dµ u and Dµ u hD,µ u vs. R, (b) Du i j and hD u,v vs. R, and (c) hτ vs. R of the 2D square lattice model, and (d), (e) and (f) the data of the 2D triangular lattice model.
